coredata.dc:title: A soy protein-based adhesive with improved mechanical and electromagnetic shielding properties by employment of core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI fillers
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Yanqiang
Jiang, Shuaicheng
Li, Jiongjiong
Tosin Aladejana, John
Zhang, Tiantian
Li, Xiaona
Dong, Youming
Li, Jianzhang
coredata.dc:description: Resolving indoor electromagnetic interference (EMI) and radiation pollution caused by electromagnetic waves have become paramount. Plywood is broadly used in interior decoration and give them EMI shielding function will be significant for addressing these issues. Herein, inspired by the mussel underwater bonding strategy and oyster, polydopamine (PDA) graft transition layer was introduced between inorganic BaTiO3 (BT) core layer and organic polyaniline (PANI) shell layer to construct controllable core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. The epoxide polymer and aldehyde-decorated carbon dots (CDs) were used as crosslinkers to form the covalent bonds and dynamic covalent bonds within the adhesive network. The core@double-shell nanoparticles were used as a reinforcing element and crosslinker to construct an organic–inorganic hybrid protein adhesive. This as-prepared protein adhesive exhibits remarkable improvements in wet shear strength (1.16 MPa), dry shear strength (1.97 MPa), and water resistance. Encouragingly, the plywood with in-situ formulated adhesives is an absorption dominated EMI shielding material and reveals an excellent EMI SET of 38.04 dB EMI in the X-band, significantly outperforming most of the reported synthetic materials. This outstanding performance is attributable to its superior conductivity and unique hierarchical structure of plywood. Therefore, this study offers a facile, environmentally-friendly, and economically feasible strategy for preparing high-performance adhesives to address indoor EMI problems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Soy protein-based adhesive
BT@PDA@PANI
Electromagnetic interference shielding
Organic-inorganic hybrid nanostructure
Plywood

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI
Structural analyses of the SM-based adhesives
Thermal and flame-retardant properties of the SM-based adhesives

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Bionic design, fabrication process, and application of SM-based adhesives in EMI shielding.

Characterizations of BT and BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. (a) FTIR and (b) XRD spectra of BT and BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. (c) UV–vis absorption spectra of PANI and BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. (d) TGA and (e) particle size analysis of BT and BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. (f) Fabrication processes of the BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. (g) Schematic illustration of the structures of BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles.

Characterizations of SM-based adhesives. (a) FTIR spectra, (b) XRD spectra, and (c) rheological properties of different SM-based adhesives. (d) Schematic illustration of the molecular interactions in SM-based adhesives.

(a) Dry and (b) wet shear strengths of the SM-based adhesives. (c) Comparison of the dry and wet shear strengths of the prepared SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive and other reported adhesives. (d) Moisture uptake values, (e) residual rates, and (f) apparent toughness and cracks on the surfaces of the SM-based adhesives; 1: SM, 2: SM/TTE, 3: SM/CDs, 4: SM/BT@PDA@PANI, 5: SM/TTE/ BT@PDA@PANI, 6: SM/CDs/ BT@PDA@PANI, 7: SM/TTE/CDs/ BT@PDA@PANI. (g) Strengthening and toughening mechanisms of SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive.

(a) TGA curves, (b) DTG curves, (c) total heat releases (THRs), (d) peak heat release rate (HRR) of the SM-based adhesives. (e) The alcohol lamp burning experiment photos for the SM-based adhesives. (f) Conductivities of SM-based adhesives.

(a) EMI SET plots of plywood composites bonded using SM-based adhesives. (b) Relationship between SET values and plywood thickness and number of plywood layers. (c) SER, SEA, and SET values of plywood composites in the X-band. (d) The ratio of SEA/SET of plywood composites in the X-band. (e) Calculated EMI shielding coefficients of plywood composites in the X-band. (f) EMI shielding performance of SBP2 and different EMI shielding materials, such as metal-, graphene-, CNT-, carbon-, and MXene-based materials. (For details, please see Table S3). (g) Radar diagram of the evaluation of most common EMI shielding materials production approaches in terms of filler costs (FC), total chemical involvement (TC), hazardous involvement (H), temperature requirement (T), equipment (E), pre/post treatment requirement (PT), and total steps (TS) (Evaluation details and references in Table S6). (h) EMI shielding mechanism diagram. 1: SM, 2: SBP1, 3: SBP2, 4: SBP3, 5: SBP4, 6: SBP5. The formulations of different plywood composites are given in Table 1 and Figure S5.
The calculated values of physical properties of plywood composites.

head.ce:title: A soy protein-based adhesive with improved mechanical and electromagnetic shielding properties by employment of core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI fillers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, No. 159 Longpan Road, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, No. 159 Longpan Road, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210037, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Wood Material Science and Application (Beijing Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Wood Material Science and Application (Beijing Forestry University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Wood Material Science and Application (Beijing Forestry University), Ministry of Education, Beijing 100083, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, No. 159 Longpan Road, Nanjing, Jiangsu 210037, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resolving indoor electromagnetic interference (EMI) and radiation pollution caused by electromagnetic waves have become paramount. Plywood is broadly used in interior decoration and give them EMI shielding function will be significant for addressing these issues. Herein, inspired by the mussel underwater bonding strategy and oyster, polydopamine (PDA) graft transition layer was introduced between inorganic BaTiO3 (BT) core layer and organic polyaniline (PANI) shell layer to construct controllable core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. The epoxide polymer and aldehyde-decorated carbon dots (CDs) were used as crosslinkers to form the covalent bonds and dynamic covalent bonds within the adhesive network. The core@double-shell nanoparticles were used as a reinforcing element and crosslinker to construct an organic–inorganic hybrid protein adhesive. This as-prepared protein adhesive exhibits remarkable improvements in wet shear strength (1.16 MPa), dry shear strength (1.97 MPa), and water resistance. Encouragingly, the plywood with in-situ formulated adhesives is an absorption dominated EMI shielding material and reveals an excellent EMI SET of 38.04 dB EMI in the X-band, significantly outperforming most of the reported synthetic materials. This outstanding performance is attributable to its superior conductivity and unique hierarchical structure of plywood. Therefore, this study offers a facile, environmentally-friendly, and economically feasible strategy for preparing high-performance adhesives to address indoor EMI problems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of fifth-generation communication technology and high-frequency electronic devices, indoor electromagnetic interference (EMI) and radiation pollution are growing more and more serious [1], which do harm to human health and the housing environments, especially to the older population and children [2–4]. To address this issue, designing high-efficiency EMI shielding materials is a promising route [5,6]. Recently, commercial EMI shielding composites have been produced by integrating polymers with conductive and/or magnetic fillers, such as metals [7,8], carbon materials [9,10], conductive polymers [11,12], and ceramic fillers [13]. For instance, Xie and co-workers have produced ​a novel Ti3C2Tx/RGO/ANFs hybrid aerogel for efficient electromagnetic interference shielding along with good mechanical properties [14]. Zhuo et al. prepared polyimide-based PI@MXene@ANFs/PI hybrid foams assembled with MXene and polyimide bilayer coatings via a facile strategy of freeze-drying plus thermal imidization followed by two-step dip-coating [15]. However, the EMI shielding efficiency strongly relies on the mass addition of conductive fillers to form a continuous conductive network within the matrix [13,16]. Consequently, such materials generally suffer from unfavorable trade-off between their EMI shielding and mechanical performance [17,18]. In addition, it is urgently needed and of great significance to develop large-area EMI shielding materials to mitigate indoor electromagnetic pollution.

Plywood is widely used as interior decoration materials, such as wainscoting, doors, and windows [19]. It would be of great value to give plywood EMI shielding function, which could efficiently minimize the problem associated with indoor electromagnetic pollution. Significantly, plywood with EMI shielding performance can be manufactured via a simple one-step hot-pressing process by using EMI shielding adhesives. Due to its unique structure, multiple division plates with EMI shielding could be created. Unfortunately, approximately 90 % of wood adhesives are formaldehyde-based [20], which release carcinogenic formaldehyde during their applications [21]. To solve the problem of formaldehyde pollution, soy protein-based adhesives have been attempted to produce plywood since they are renewable, readily available, and nontoxic [22]. However, soy protein-based adhesives have inferior strength and weak water resistance [23], which severely limits their industrial applications and weakens the significance and potentials of developing EMI shielding protein adhesives.

Protein adhesives can be strengthened and toughened through the construction of organic–inorganic hybrid system [24,25]. BaTiO3 (BT) is a rigid inorganic nanofiller for ferroelectric ceramics with electromagnetic waves (EMW) absorption capacity [26], which can be strengthened and toughened through the nanoparticles size effect. The integration of BT nanoparticles with protein adhesives to construct an organic–inorganic hybrid structure is expected to impart adhesives many functions, such as improved water-resistant adhesion performance and remarkable EMI shielding property [27]. However, it is difficult to disperse unmodified inorganic BT nanoparticles because of their small sizes, high surface area, and high surface energy, thereby forming stress concentrations and disconnected conductive network [28]. This would subsequently reduce the reinforcement and EMI shielding effects of BT.

Therefore, enhancing the dispersity of BT nanofillers and regulating BT/polymer interfaces are the key factors to optimize the adhesion and EMI shielding performance of protein adhesives [29]. The BT nanoparticles can be directly bonded with polymers as nanoreactors due to the versatility in its rationalized design, which provides a promising direction to prepare high-strength organic–inorganic hybrid nanocomposites [30]. Polydopamine (PDA) has been broadly employed as a versatile secondary functional platform for grafting amine-rich biopolymers [31]. Designing BT core@double shell nanostructures using PDA as a tough interlayer could advance the strengthening efficiency of BT [32], which will exhibit extraordinary interfacial binding effects even in small loading content. Furthermore, it is also necessary to establish strong interfacial interactions between BT core–shell nanoparticles and protein chains for superior energy absorption capacity and damage tolerance, such as covalent crosslinks [33,34]. The aldehyde-decorated carbon dots (CDs) can be used as a promising crosslinker alternative to interact with amino-rich compounds through Schiff base reactions [35,36].

In this paper, inspired by mussel adhesion proteins and oyster, a PDA graft transition layer was introduced between the inorganic BT core layer and the organic polyaniline (PANI) shell layer. The BT@PDA@PANI hybrid nanostructures were designed to form a nanoparticle-dispersed but interpenetrating conductive network structure through a tighter grafting reaction between these two layers. The core@double-shell nanoparticles were used as a reinforcing element and crosslinker to construct an organic–inorganic hybrid protein adhesive with soybean meal (SM) and CDs. The epoxide polymer and aldehyde-decorated carbon dots (CDs) were used as strong crosslinkers to form the covalent bonds and dynamic covalent bonds within the adhesive network. This as-prepared protein adhesive exhibits enhanced strength, water resistance, and flame retardancy. Moreover, the multiple division plates with shielding EMI shielding adhesives were naturally and facilely manufactured due to the unique structure of the plywood assembled layer by layer. The electromagnetic waves could be efficiently absorbed repetitively between the layers of the plywood (EMI shielding layers). Therefore, an excellent EMI shielding and mechanical performance were synchronously achieved despite introducing little BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. These results open new possibilities for the practical applications of BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles in EMI shielding high‐performance adhesives.

SM (56 % protein content) was obtained from Yuwang Ecological Food Industry Co., ltd. (Shandong, China). Aniline, ammonium persulfate (APS), and barium titanate (BT, < 100 nm) were provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Trimethylolpropane triglycidyl ethe (TTE, Epoxy value/100 g: 0.70) and other chemicals were supplied by Nanjing Chemical Reagents Co., ltd. (Nanjing, China).

The surface modification of BT nanoparticles was facilitated simply by immersing the BT in a dilute dopamine buffer solution. Briefly, 10 g of BT nanoparticles were dispersed in a Tris-HCl (0.05 M, 200 mL) buffer solution at pH 8.5 and ultrasonicated for 25 min in an ice-water bath. Then, 0.38 g of dopamine hydrochloride was added and the mixture was gently stirred at 25 °C for 24 h. The crude mixture was centrifuged and thoroughly rinsed with ethanol for three times. Finally, the resulting mixture was dried at 60 °C for 48 h to yield the BT@PDA core–shell nanoparticles. An in-situ polymerization method was used to coat PANI onto the surface of BT@PDA particles. In a typical experiment, 5 g of BT@PDA was dispersed in 50 mL of HCl (1 M), followed with the addition of 0.91 mL of aniline and 0.001 g of CTAB. The mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min and then stirred for 30 min at 1 °C. After cooling down, another precooled 50 mL of HCl (1 M) containing 0.57 g of APS was poured. The molar ratio of aniline/APS was 4:1. The reaction was kept at 1 °C for 4 h with constant stirring, and the mixture gradually turned dark green. The nanoparticles were then washed several times with deionized water and ethanol before dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h. Finally, the core-double-shell BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles were obtained.

Glutaraldehyde (600 µL) and ethanol (300 µL) were added to a pressure vessel and heated in an oven at 150 °C for 2 h. After being cooled down, the resultant brown solid was redispersed in acetone and chloroform and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to remove high‐weight carbon aggregates. The whole procedure was repeated three times for each solvent and finally the solution was evaporated under reduced pressure to obtain a brown solid.

First, distilled water (72 g) and soybean meal (SM) (28 g) were put in a beaker and stirred with a cantilever electric stirring paddle at a constant speed of 800 rpm for 20 min to evenly disperse the soybean meal (SM)-based adhesive. TTE (3 wt%) was put in SM-based adhesive and stirred evenly to obtain SM/TTE adhesive. CDs (3 wt%) was put in SM-based adhesive and stirred evenly to prepare SM/CDs adhesive. BT@PDA@PANI (0.5 wt%) was put in the SM-based adhesive and stirred evenly to prepare SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive. The formulations of different adhesives are given in Table S1.

The adhesive bonding performance was assessed by testing the shear strength of the resultant three-ply plywood using a universal testing machine (AGS-X, Japan) based on the China Standard GB/T 17657–2013. On a poplar veneer (400 × 400 × 1 mm), the prepared adhesive (180 g/m2) was uniformly distributed. After 320 s of hot pressing at 120 °C and 1 MPa, the plywood was kept at 25 °C and 50 percent relative humidity for 48 h. The shear strength of the prepared plywood (100 mm × 25 mm × 3 mm) was measured following the China National Standard (GB/T 17657–2013). The dry and wet shear strengths were measured by using a universal tensile machine (AGS-X, Japan) at a loading speed of 20 mm/min. For the wet shear strength test, the plywood was boiled at 63 °C for 3 h, and maintained at room temperature for 20 min. Each group was tested six times.

The viscosity of the SM-based adhesive was measured using an RST-CPS rheometer with a shear rate of 0.2 s−1 at 25 °C, and the viscosity tests were performed five times for each adhesive sample and calculated the average value.
The slide was evenly smeared with the prepared adhesive, and surface cracks were observed after complete curing at 120 ℃.

The SM-based adhesives were placed in a 120 °C oven to obtain a constant weight (m1). Then, the adhesives were immersed in the water at room temperature for 24 h. After that, the adhesives were oven dried at 105 °C for 3 h until a constant weight (m2) was obtained. The residual rates were calculated according to the residual mass ratio of m2/m1 (%). Six replicates were performed for each adhesive sample.

The SM-based adhesives were placed in a 120 ± 3 °C environment until they depicted a constant weight (m). Subsequently, at 50 °C and 80 % relative humidity, they were placed in a humidity chamber and weight monitored until they reached a constant value (M). The moisture uptake calculation formula is as follows:
Moisture
u p t a k e
r a t e
%
=
M
-
m
/ m
×
100 %
Moisture uptake measurements were performed on six identical adhesives and the average was reported.

The flame retardancy of the adhesives was tested using a micro-combustion calorimeter (MCC, FTT, England). In addition, a 2 mm thick adhesive sample was exposed to the external flame of an alcohol lamp, and the fire resistance of the adhesive was assessed by burning tests. The thermogravimetric analysis of the SM-based adhesive was evaluated using a Q50 thermogravimetric analyzer (TA instruments, USA). The samples were tested in a platinum cup and the weight losses were recorded while heating at 10 °C/min under a nitrogen environment.

The SM-based hybrid adhesive and plywood in the X-band were tested using a microwave network vector analyzer (Agilent Technologies E5071C). Samples measuring 22.9 × 10.2 mm were used for the evaluation. The EMI shielding effect was evaluated based on the S parameters (S11 and S21), measured using the waveguide method and calculated using the following formula.
S
E T
=
S
E R
+ S
E A
+ S
E M
S
E R
= - 10 lg
1 - R
= - 10 lg
1 -
S 11
2
S
E A
=
-
10 lg
T / 1 - R
=
-
10 lg
S 21
2
/ 1 -
S 11
2
The SET is the sum of reflection (SER), absorption (SEA), and multiple reflections (SEM).

Attenuated total reflection Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) were recorded on a Nicolet iS50 infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., ltd, USA) with the background from 400 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 at ambient temperature for 32 scans. Chemical structure analysis of SM-based adhesives was performed using X-ray diffraction patterns (XRD, Ultima IV analyzer, Rigaku, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI
ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication processes of the BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 2f. The polymerization reactions of dopamine (DOPA) took place on the surface of the BT nanoparticles, which can interact strongly with a variety of inorganic nanoparticles by hydrogen bonding [37]. Then, PANI was immobilized on the BT@PDA surface by in-situ polymerization, and DOPA was usually used as PDA adhesive layer to improve the interfacial bonding between fillers and the polymer matrix (Fig. 2f) [38]. A possible reaction mechanism for PDA self-polymerization is shown in Figure S1. It is likely that dopamine monomers undergo cyclization via Michael addition to form quinones and oxidation first. Then the 5,6-dihydroxyindole monomers were covalently crosslinked via aryl-aryl linkages to form PDA. The physicochemical properties of the nanoparticles were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray diffraction (XRD), ultra violet (UV), and thermogravimetric analysis (TGA). As revealed in Fig. 2a, the absorption band at 561 cm−1 was assigned to the Ti-O bending vibration in BT. The apparent absorption bands at 1586 and 1497 cm−1 can be found on the FTIR spectrum of BT@PDA@PANI (Fig. 2a), which belong to the skeleton vibration of benzene rings from polyaniline [39], suggesting the presence of PANI shell in nanoparticles. The XRD patterns of BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles are shown in Fig. 2b; the BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles mainly presents typical PANI diffraction peaks centered ((010), (100), and (110)). Fig. 2c shows that the spectrum of BT@PDA@PANI exhibit two characteristic absorption bands at 330 and 620 nm, which correspond to the π − π* and n − π* transitions of the benzenoid and quinoid rings of PANI, respectively [40]. The results of FTIR, XRD, and UV proved the successful preparation of core@double-shell structured BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles.

We further characterized the nanostructure of the nanoparticles by TGA, SEM, TEM, and laser nanoparticle size analysis. The TGA was used to characterize the quantity of PDA@PANI coated on the surfaces of the BT. The neat weight loss of functionalized BT@PDA@PANI was calculated to be 3.2 % (Fig. 2d), while the weight loss of the unmodified BT was essentially nonexistent. Hence this value was attributable to the coated PDA@PANI. The average diameter measured by a laser nanoparticle size analysis was around 213.4 nm (Fig. 2e). The increase of the weight loss and average diameter further prove the successful preparation of core@double-shell structured BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. The morphology and nanostructure of the as-synthesized nanoparticles were characterized by SEM and TEM. Notably, the PDA shell with a thickness of about 2–5 nm was uniformly coated on the surface of BT nanoparticles (about 100 nm, Figure S2). Then, a layer of PANI shell was coated on the outside of BT@PDA nanoparticles, with a total thickness of about 5–10 nm (Figure S3). All BT nanoparticles were fully incorporated in the polymer without debonding or aggregation and were uniformly encased in a polymer shell (Fig. 2g).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural analyses of the SM-based adhesives
ce:section.ce:para:
Figs. 1
and 3d depict the steps used to develop the SM-based adhesives, which include the uniform mixing of BT@PDA@PANI, CDs, and trimethylolpropane triglycidyl ether (TTE) with the SM matrix, followed by curing at 120 ℃. The formulations of different adhesives are given in Table S1. The densely crosslinked networks are produced in the cured adhesive system by multiple physical and chemical interactions. This could result in an improvement in the bonding strength and water resistance of the adhesive [41,42]. In addition, BT@PDA@PANI was used as an inorganic filler to form an organic–inorganic hybrid structure with protein chains by intermolecular hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions [39]. The adhesives with BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles are renowned for their high conductivity, which is advantageous for reflecting and absorbing electromagnetic waves (EMWs) in EMI shielding materials [43,44]. The CDs were utilized as a dopant and small-molecule crosslinker for BT@PDA@PANI and protein chains to form a supramolecular network structure within adhesives. This completely interconnected network optimizes the reinforcing efficiencies of the stiff organic–inorganic hybrid nanostructures, which is advantageous for enhancing the adhesives' mechanical strength.

To examine the molecular interactions in SM-based adhesives, a variety of characterizations were performed. Fig. 3 a shows two peaks at 1482 and 1560 cm−1 for the PANI, which correspond to the benzenoid and quinoid rings in BT@PDA@PANI. In addition, the 1036 cm−1 peak in BT@PDA@PANI is due to the in-plane deformation of N H bonds. The peak at 1036 cm−1 shifted to 1045 cm−1, whereas the peak at 1702 cm−1 shifted to 1697 cm−1 [45,46]. The change in the peak location of the − OH bending vibration to a higher wavenumber was attributed to the creation of hydrogen bonds. These results indicate the strong interfacial interactions (hydrogen bonding, covalent bonging, and electrostatic interactions) between the BT@PDA@PANI and protein chains networks.

In addition, compared with SM adhesive, the absorption peak of amide II (NH bending) at 1402 cm−1 of SM/CDs and SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive were significantly weakened. The appearance of characteristic vibration bands of CN at 1612 cm−1, indicating the Schiff base (CN bonds) was formed by the crosslinking of the amine groups of protein chains with the aldehyde groups of CDs [47]. After the reaction with BT@PDA@PANI and protein chains, the stretching absorption band of free aldehyde (1722 cm−1) of CDs vanishes. This phenomenon illustrates the Maillard reaction between CDs, BT@PDA@PANI, and protein chains with full consumption of aldehyde groups [48]. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 4 f, changes in the apparent color of the resultant adhesives further provide evidence for the Maillard reaction. The apparent color changed from pale yellow for neat SM adhesive to dark brown for SM/CDs adhesive. Fig. 3b shows that the diffraction patterns of different adhesives have two large peaks at 2θ = 8.8° and 19.8°. In the spectrum of the adhesive, the intensity of the characteristic peaks at 2θ = 19.8° gradually decreased and when compared to SM adhesive. This is due to the fact that the crosslinking reactions messed with the random arrangement of protein chains and caused more densely crosslinked networks to emerge. These crosslinked networks made it difficult to establish an ordered structure during the curing phase [49]. Fig. 3c displays the rheological characteristics of SM-based adhesives. The viscosity value of the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive is higher than that of the SM adhesive. This indicates that strong interfacial interactions (hydrogen bonding, covalent bonding, and electrostatic interactions) formed multiple crosslinking networks between protein chains, CDs, and BT@PDA@PANI (Fig. 3d).

Mechanical properties are critical indicators for adhesives application, which are important issues that should be considered. The organic–inorganic integrity and multiple network structures are advantageous to improve the mechanical properties of the adhesives. As shown in Fig. 4, the dry and wet shear strengths of neat SM adhesive are approximately 1.04 ± 0.1 and 0.34 ± 0.05 MPa, respectively. With the incorporation of core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI, the dry and wet shear strengths of the modified SM-based adhesive increased dramatically. This is because BT@PDA@PANI can be uniformly distributed in an adhesive network. Moreover, the BT@PDA@PANI contains various functional groups that can interact with the adhesive network through hydrogen bonding, covalent bonding, and electrostatic interactions [50]. Compared with the pristine SM adhesive, the wet shear strength of SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive attained 1.16 ± 0.1 MPa, which is 241.2 % higher than that of the SM adhesive. This result was due to the formation of the stable crosslinked network and organic–inorganic hybrid structure after the incorporation of the core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. As evidenced by the FTIR analysis, the BT@PDA@PANI forms multiple interfacial interactions with protein chains and CDs, which is advantageous for building a robust crosslinked system that can withstand external damage [51–53]. Notably, the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive exhibits mechanical qualities that are superior to those of the majority of previously reported bio-based adhesives (e.g., protein-, tannin-, lignin-, and starch-based adhesives) (Fig. 4c and Table S2) [22,42,54–56].

The influence of CDs and BT@PDA@PANI on the water resistance of the formulated SM-based adhesives was further explored. As shown in Fig. 4d and 4e, the water resistance of the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive was significantly improved. This demonstrates that BT@PDA@PANI can be incorporated into the adhesive network and deployed evenly across the system. The toughness of the SM-based adhesives was determined by examining fractures in the cured adhesive films. As illustrated in Fig. 4f, the SM adhesive exhibits several holes and splits. In contrast, the surfaces of the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive were nearly completely homogenous, thus proving the excellent toughness of the adhesive. This phenomenon attributes to an amalgamation of massive shear yielding, crazing, and nano cavitation in the highly interconnected 3D polymer network [57].

In view of the above structural characteristics and analysis, a mechanism was proposed (Fig. 4g) to explain the unique mechanical behavior of SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive. Firstly, the adhesive was improved by the homogeneous dispersion of the core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles in the matrix [54]. Secondly, the core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles self-assembled to form a uniform organic–inorganic hierarchical structure during the adhesive curing process, realizing multiscale nano toughening mechanisms in which nanoparticles bridged two crack surfaces [58]. Finally, covalent bonding, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions between BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles, CDs and protein chains effectively improve energy dissipation under stretching based on a robust organic–inorganic hierarchical structure [59]. Meanwhile, the mechanical properties of the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive were greatly improved by the dynamic interfacial interactions to form interpenetrated networks between the protein chains, CDs, and BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles that thoroughly interpenetrated networks.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal and flame-retardant properties of the SM-based adhesives
ce:section.ce:para: The TGA curves of SM-based adhesives are shown in Fig. 5 a, and the free water in all samples was removed by heating at 203.2 °C. The main weight loss of SM-based adhesives occurs throughout 210–490 °C (Fig. 5a). The maximum mass loss rate of SM adhesive is at 320.8 °C, while that of the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive shifts to higher temperature of 333.2 °C (Fig. 5b). Compared with the SM adhesive with 89.9 % weight loss, the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive has a considerably small weight loss of 72.4 %. In addition, the TGA results indicate that the thermal stability of the adhesive could be improved due to the presence of BT@PDA@PANI, and the formation of robust organic–inorganic hybrid nanostructure within the SM-based adhesive matrix. Next, the flame-retardant of the SM-based adhesives was investigated using the heat release rate (HRR) and total heat release (THR). As shown in Fig. 5c, the THR and PHRR of SM (9.5 kJ/g, 70.4 W/g) are far beyond that of SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive (7.6 kJ/g, 56 W/g). Meanwhile, the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive also surpasses the peak HRR temperature of its counterparts (Fig. 5d). Furthermore, the presented combustion tests affirmed the improvement in flame retardancy of the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive (Fig. 5e and Figure S4). The remarkably improved flame retardancy of the adhesive could be attributable to the fact that the introduced BT@PDA@PANI can effectively reduce the oxygen diffusion from the heat source to the adhesive matrix and slow down the heating accumulation to delay the initiation point of burning, thereby reducing the combustion kinetics. Simultaneously, due to the robust organic–inorganic hierarchical structure, demonstrating the great advantage of the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive in flame retardancy. Furthermore, the addition of TTE and CDs are conducive to the formation of interpenetrating multiple networks with strong interface interaction. Therefore, the dense crosslinked heat conduction network improves the heat conduction performance and makes the heat conduction easier [60].

In order to assess the EMI shielding mechanism of SBP plywood, the contributions from the coefficients of reflection (R), absorption (A), and transmission (T) were further investigated and analyzed. As depicted in Fig. 6e, EMI SE is an absorption dominated EMI shielding material with low reflection characteristics to prevent secondary EMI pollution resulting from microwave reflection. Fig. 6f shows that the SBP2 has superior EMI shielding capability compared to other materials in the literature, including MXene-, metal-, and carbon-based materials [64,65]. In addition, SBP2 exhibits a relatively high SET/ adhesive thickness (SE/t) value of 5513 dB cm−1 (Table S3).

Based on the above evidence, a possible EMI shielding mechanism of SBP plywood was proposed, as shown in Fig. 6h. Firstly, incident waves are absorbed by the polarization loss generated by the interface polarization of BT@PDA@PANI nanoparticles. Secondly, when the EMWs reach the surface of the SBP plywood, due to the unique hierarchical structure of plywood, it is possible to create a multi split panel with EMI shielding by bonding the surface in situ. Consequently, the remaining EMWs have an energy drop after passing through the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive layers. Finally, the surviving EMWs contact the subsequent adhesive layers, where they will suffer repeated internal reflections until they are completely absorbed.

The cost of the developed material is assessed in the light of their promising applications of as EMI shielding materials. In addition to the matrix, SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive mainly comprises commercial inexpensive raw materials, the synthesis core@double-shell BT@PDA@PANI (50480 RMB/ton), TTE (15000 RMB/ton), CDs (24000 RMB/ton), and the SM (5500 RMB/ton), and thus cost of adhesive is as low as 2532 RMB/ton (see Supplementary Note 1, Table S4). Simultaneously, the entire preparation procedure only requires synthesis BT@PDA@PANI and stirring at room temperature. In addition, the cost of prepared SBP2 EMI shielding material with the best effect in our work includes 6 layers of adhesive (180 g/m2) is 2165 RMB/m3 (see Supplementary Note 1, Table S5). A radar diagram was used to evaluate processing approaches to EMI shielding materials. The rating system of the radar diagram was given in Supplementary Note 2. The larger area of the radar diagram indicates more competition in industrial production. As shown in the Fig. 6g (Supplementary Table S6), it can be seen that compared with recently reported approaches, the EMI shielding materials prepared by our approach do not rely on complex equipment or high temperature, which is more promising for large-scale industrial production.

In summary, by rationally designing nanostructured inorganic fillers, a simple and environmentally-friendly technique was developed for synthesizing multifunctional soy protein-based adhesives, which featured outstanding EMI shielding, conductivity, water resistance, and flame retardancy. Under the action of 0.5 wt% BT@PDA@PANI, the adhesive realized the construction of multiple crosslinked organic–inorganic hybrid nanostructures, and achieved the aim of toughening and strength. The resulting SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive has a high wet shear strength (1.16 MPa) as well as a high dry shear strength (1.97 MPa), which are 89.4 % and 241.2 % higher than that of the SM adhesive, respectively. Moreover, the SET of the plywood bonded with the SM/TTE/CDs/BT@PDA@PANI adhesive is 38.04 dB, significantly outperforming most of the reported synthetic materials. Therefore, this study offers an economical, eco-friendly, and feasible strategy for preparing high-performance soy protein-based adhesives to address EMI problems associated with advanced materials.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32101456 and 31901253), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20200795), and the Postgraduate Research & Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (No. KYCX21_0896 and KYCX21_0890).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.141512.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
